A Roofing Sub That Works Inside Your Schedule

html:
Roofing Large-Span Assembly and Supplier Plants Along I-85

Spartanburg County sits inside one of the densest automotive supply chains in the Southeast, centered on a major assembly operation in Greer and threaded together by Tier 1 and Tier 2 supplier plants strung along I-85 and the adjoining industrial parks. We build and maintain roofs on that inventory, and the scope on these buildings looks nothing like a strip retail reroof.

What Sets an Automotive-Grade Roof Apart

Assembly and supplier buildings run large, often several hundred thousand square feet under one membrane, with process equipment, paint operations, and material-handling traffic embedded directly into the roof plan. The facilities engineering group typically has a production calendar that does not move for a roofing crew, so the project plan has to move around it instead.

We start every automotive-sector bid by reviewing the plant's shift schedule and any planned production downtime windows before proposing a sequence, because the roof plan and the plant's operating calendar have to be the same document.

Supplier plants themselves range from newer pre-engineered buildings put up specifically to serve a nearby assembly customer to older manufacturing space repurposed for automotive work as the supply chain expanded. We treat these differently at the estimate stage, since a newer pre-engineered building and a converted general manufacturing building carry different deck conditions, different existing warranty status, and different structural assumptions going into a project.

Sequencing Tear-Off Across a Large Deck

A deck this size cannot be opened all at once over a running plant. We section the field into zones sized to what a crew can tear off, dry in, and close out inside a single shift, keeping crane staging and material storage inside the zone boundary so adjacent production areas stay undisturbed.

Dry-in status gets confirmed and logged at the end of every shift before crews leave the roof, and that log becomes part of the closeout package the plant's maintenance department keeps on file.

Hot-Work Permitting Near Paint and Finishing Operations

Paint booths and finishing lines carry solvent vapor and fire-suppression requirements that restrict torch, grinder, and open-flame work on the roof zones above and adjacent to them. We submit a hot-work permit request to the plant's EHS department before scheduling that portion of the job, and where permits restrict open flame we specify cold-applied adhesive or mechanically fastened attachment instead of torch-applied modified bitumen.

We also confirm evacuation and fire watch requirements tied to any approved hot-work permit before scheduling, since a plant's EHS department typically requires a dedicated fire watch for a set period after any open-flame activity near combustible materials, and that staffing has to be built into the project schedule and cost rather than treated as an afterthought once the crew is already on the roof.

Vibration Loads From Stamping and Press Operations

Stamping presses and heavy machining put a vibration signature into the building structure that a standard commercial seam design is not built to absorb. On press-adjacent roof zones we tighten the welding and seam-inspection procedure and specify fastening patterns rated for the higher cyclic load, rather than applying the same detail used on the office or shipping-side roof areas of the same building.

Wind Uplift Across an Unbroken Field

A single, unbroken membrane field this large is a significant wind load event waiting for the right storm, and Spartanburg County sees enough severe thunderstorm activity through the spring and summer to test it. Uplift pressure concentrates at corners and perimeters, so we calculate the fastening pattern by exposure category and building height under ASCE 7 rather than running one fastener density across the whole field, and we detail edge metal and coping to resist the peel-back that starts most large-field wind failures.

We also review existing parapet height and coping condition on older supplier buildings before specifying an edge detail, since a low parapet built to an earlier code cycle sometimes needs additional blocking or a taller edge metal profile to meet current uplift requirements on a reroof.

  • Manufacturer spec sheet matched to the deck type and existing insulation, submitted with every bid
  • FM Approvals or UL-listed assembly documentation where the plant's insurer requires it
  • Hot-work permit coordination for paint-shop and finishing-line zones
  • Zone-by-zone tear-off sequencing with logged daily dry-in confirmation
  • Wind uplift calculation under ASCE 7 for the building's specific exposure category
  • NDL warranty registration and as-built roof-zone diagram at closeout

Closeout Documentation Plants Actually Need

Automotive and supplier facilities run their own internal audit and insurance-renewal processes, and the roof closeout package has to feed into that rather than sit in a drawer. We deliver warranty registration, an as-built roof-zone and penetration diagram, daily production logs from the job, and a photographed condition survey formatted the way the plant's facilities engineering department requests it, not a generic template.

Automotive Plant Roofing Questions

Can you work without shutting down an active production line?

Yes. We plan zone boundaries and staging specifically to avoid interrupting lines that stay active during the project, and we confirm those boundaries with the facilities engineering group before work starts.

How do you handle hot-work restrictions near paint operations?

We request EHS approval before scheduling any torch, grinder, or open-flame work near paint or finishing areas, and we switch to cold adhesive or mechanical attachment where a permit restricts open flame.

What membrane do you typically specify on large assembly and supplier roofs?

Mechanically attached TPO or PVC in the 60- to 80-mil range is common on large open fields, with fully adhered application in zones where fastener patterns conflict with hot-work restrictions.

Do you provide documentation for the plant's insurance carrier?

We provide the manufacturer warranty, assembly listing, and inspection records the carrier typically requests. Where a carrier needs a specific format, we prepare it to that standard.

How fast can you respond if a seam opens during a storm?

We treat manufacturing accounts as priority mobilization for temporary dry-in, since an open deck over active production or inventory escalates quickly.
